name: Linear Programming
plain_description: A method for finding the best way to distribute limited resources (money, staff, supplies) across multiple uses when both the goal and the limits can be expressed as straight-line relationships. Think of it as finding the fairest and most effective way to divide a pie when you know exactly how each slice will be used.
signals: Allocating a fixed budget across programmes or sites
Distributing limited supplies (food, medicine, equipment) to multiple locations
Deciding how many staff or volunteers to assign to different tasks
Maximising total reach or impact given a spending cap
All decisions and constraints involve simple proportional relationships (no binary yes/no choices)
contraindications: Decisions are binary (fund or don't fund) — use Integer Programming instead
Multiple conflicting objectives (cost vs. equity vs. impact) — use Multi-objective Optimisation
Uncertainty or unknown probabilities are central — use Simulation or POMDP
social_examples: A food bank deciding how many kilograms of each food type to send to 15 partner organisations, given each organisation's capacity and nutritional needs
A health department allocating a fixed vaccine supply across clinics to maximise immunisation coverage
An NGO splitting its annual grant across 8 programme areas to maximise beneficiaries reached
data_requirements: Objective coefficients (e.g., impact per dollar spent at each site)
Constraint coefficients (e.g., capacity of each site, total budget)
Lower and upper bounds on each decision variable
reference_paper: Dantzig, G.B. (1963). Linear Programming and Extensions. Princeton University Press.

name: Integer Programming
plain_description: An extension of resource allocation math for situations where decisions are all-or-nothing: open this shelter or not, fund this project or not, place a worker in this district or not. It finds the best combination of binary choices within your constraints.
signals: Choosing which subset of locations, projects, or programmes to fund or open
Deciding which communities to serve given a limit on how many you can reach
Selecting which interventions to run when you can only run a fixed number
Facility location: where to open new offices, clinics, or distribution hubs
Binary or integer quantity decisions (can't send half a worker)
contraindications: All decisions are continuous proportions — use Linear Programming instead
The number of possible combinations is astronomically large without clear structure — consider heuristics or simulation
social_examples: A microfinance institution selecting which 20 of 80 loan applicants to fund, given a fixed capital pool
An NGO deciding which 5 of 12 districts to operate in, given staff capacity limits
A health ministry choosing which combination of interventions to fund within a fixed annual budget
data_requirements: Value or impact of each option if selected
Cost or resource consumption of each option
Total budget or capacity constraint
Any logical constraints between options (e.g., if A is selected, B must also be)
reference_paper: Nemhauser, G.L. & Wolsey, L.A. (1988). Integer and Combinatorial Optimization. Wiley.

name: Multi-objective Optimisation
plain_description: A method for finding the best solutions when you have two or more goals that pull in different directions — for example, maximising impact while minimising cost AND ensuring equity across communities. Rather than one 'best' answer, it produces a set of trade-off options (a Pareto frontier) so decision-makers can choose based on their values.
signals: Two or more objectives that cannot all be maximised simultaneously
Need to explicitly explore the trade-off between cost and impact
Equity or fairness is a stated goal alongside efficiency
Stakeholders disagree on which objective matters most
Decision-makers want to see a range of options, not just one answer
contraindications: Only one objective matters — use LP or IP instead
Objectives can be combined into a single weighted score with agreed weights — use LP with a composite objective
social_examples: A city housing authority balancing cost-per-unit, geographic equity, and speed of delivery for a housing voucher programme
A global health funder exploring the trade-off between DALYs saved, cost-effectiveness, and coverage of marginalised groups
An education NGO balancing student outcomes, cost, and geographic reach when choosing which schools to support
data_requirements: Quantified measure for each objective
Decision variables and constraints (same as LP/IP)
Stakeholder preferences or value weights (optional, for post-processing)
reference_paper: Deb, K. et al. (2002). A Fast and Elitist Multiobjective Genetic Algorithm: NSGA-II. IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation.

name: Network / Routing Optimisation
plain_description: A family of methods for problems involving movement through a network: routing vehicles or workers, designing supply chains, or distributing resources through connected nodes. Finds the fastest, cheapest, or most impactful paths and assignments across a geographic or organisational network.
signals: Workers, vehicles, or supplies need to travel between multiple locations
Finding the shortest or cheapest route between points
Designing which locations should supply which other locations
Scheduling multiple vehicles or workers to cover a set of locations
Supply chain design: where to place warehouses or hubs
contraindications: No physical movement involved — use LP or matching instead
Network structure is unknown or must be inferred — use social network analysis first
social_examples: A health NGO routing community health workers across 30 villages to maximise patient visits per week
A food relief organisation designing supply routes from 3 warehouses to 50 distribution points
An ambulance service optimising vehicle placement to minimise average response time
data_requirements: Geographic locations (coordinates or distance matrix)
Travel times or costs between locations
Demand at each location (e.g., number of patients, kg of food needed)
Vehicle or worker capacity and availability
reference_paper: Toth, P. & Vigo, D. (2014). Vehicle Routing: Problems, Methods, and Applications. SIAM.

name: Constraint Satisfaction / Scheduling
plain_description: Methods for finding schedules or assignments that satisfy a complex set of rules simultaneously — who works when, which case goes to which worker, who attends which session. Unlike simple assignment, these problems have many hard rules that must all be respected at once.
signals: Scheduling volunteers, staff, or clients across time slots
Assigning cases or tasks to workers with specific qualifications
Creating timetables where many constraints must hold simultaneously
Rostering with availability, skill, and workload constraints
Tournament or session scheduling
contraindications: No time dimension and assignments are one-to-one without complex rules — use Matching instead
Optimising a single objective without hard rule constraints — use LP or IP
social_examples: A domestic violence shelter scheduling counsellors across shifts, respecting availability, mandatory rest, and case-type qualifications
An employment programme scheduling job-seekers into training sessions, respecting language needs, childcare hours, and room capacities
A refugee resettlement organisation timetabling orientation sessions across 4 sites for families with different language groups
data_requirements: Set of tasks or sessions to be assigned
Set of workers, rooms, or time slots available
Hard constraints (qualifications, availability, capacity)
Soft preferences (optional, for quality optimisation)
reference_paper: Rossi, F., van Beek, P. & Walsh, T. (2006). Handbook of Constraint Programming. Elsevier.

name: Matching / Assignment
plain_description: Methods for pairing people or resources from two groups so that the overall match quality is as high as possible. Like a compatibility algorithm, but mathematically guaranteed to find the best overall pairing — not just good individual matches.
signals: Pairing beneficiaries with services, workers, or resources
Assigning clients to case managers based on compatibility or need
Matching donors with recipients (organ donation, housing, scholarships)
Allocating students to schools or programmes
One-to-one or one-to-many pairing with preference or compatibility data
contraindications: Assignments involve complex scheduling rules — use Constraint Satisfaction instead
One side has no preferences and assignments are purely capacity-based — use LP
social_examples: A refugee resettlement organisation matching families to host communities based on language, cultural, medical, and geographic compatibility
A mentorship programme pairing at-risk youth with mentors based on shared interests and availability
A social housing agency matching applicants to available units based on need, family size, and accessibility requirements
data_requirements: Compatibility or preference scores between each pair
Capacity constraints on each entity (e.g., one case manager can take max 20 clients)
Hard eligibility rules (e.g., must speak same language)
reference_paper: Gale, D. & Shapley, L.S. (1962). College Admissions and the Stability of Marriage. American Mathematical Monthly.

name: Influence Maximisation (POMDP)
plain_description: A method for selecting the best people to involve in an intervention so that awareness, behaviour change, or information spreads as widely as possible through a social network. Particularly powerful when the social network is partially unknown. Formulated as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP), it plans across multiple rounds of interventions, learning as it goes.
signals: Selecting participants for awareness or behaviour change interventions
Maximising spread of information through a peer community
Sequential interventions where you can observe and update after each round
Network structure is uncertain or only partially known
Goal is to influence as many people as possible indirectly through direct participants
contraindications: No social network structure — the population is disconnected individuals
Intervention is one-shot with no sequential rounds
Network is fully known with certainty — simpler greedy methods may suffice
social_examples: Homeless shelters selecting youth to participate in HIV prevention workshops, so they spread awareness to peers (cf. HEALER system, Yadav et al. 2016)
Community health programmes choosing which community members to train as peer health educators
An NGO selecting which farmers to train in climate-resilient practices to maximise adoption across a rural community
data_requirements: Social network structure (friendship or trust edges between individuals)
Edge uncertainty probabilities (how confident you are each link exists)
Number of intervention rounds (T) and participants per round (K)
Propagation probability per edge
Observation model (what you learn after each intervention)
reference_paper: Yadav, A. et al. (2016). Using Social Networks to Aid Homeless Shelters: Dynamic Influence Maximization under Uncertainty. AAMAS 2016. arXiv:1602.00165

name: Simulation (Monte Carlo / Agent-Based)
plain_description: A method for testing how a policy, programme, or system behaves across thousands of simulated scenarios, capturing uncertainty and complexity that equations alone cannot represent. Monte Carlo simulation tests sensitivity to random variation; agent-based simulation models how individual actors (people, organisations) interact to produce system-level outcomes.
signals: Uncertainty in key inputs (disease prevalence, uptake rates, demand) is high
System behaviour emerges from interactions between many individuals
Testing a policy before committing resources in the real world
Wanting to understand the range of possible outcomes (best/worst/likely case)
No closed-form analytical solution is tractable
contraindications: A single best decision needs to be computed — use LP/IP instead
Uncertainty is low and the system is well-understood — simulation adds cost without insight
social_examples: A government modelling how different cash transfer amounts affect food security across 10,000 simulated household trajectories
An NGO simulating how a disease spreads through a community under different vaccination coverage scenarios before committing to a strategy
A housing authority stress-testing its placement system against scenarios of sudden refugee influxes
data_requirements: Probability distributions for uncertain inputs (not just point estimates)
Behavioural rules for agents (if agent-based)
Validation data to calibrate the model against reality
reference_paper: Axelrod, R. (1997). The Complexity of Cooperation: Agent-Based Models of Competition and Collaboration. Princeton University Press.
